name: Geophysics
description: Earth structure, seismic wave propagation, petrophysics, plate tectonics, and the physical processes that shape our planet. Geophysics applies the principles of physics to understand the Earth from its deep interior (core, mantle) to its surface expressions (volcanoes, earthquakes, mountain belts).
axioms: Seismic waves: P-waves (compressional, fastest, travel through solids and liquids) and S-waves (shear, travel only through solids)
Snell's law governs seismic ray paths through layers of varying velocity (critical for refraction/reflection seismology)
Gravity anomalies reveal subsurface density variations — corrections for latitude, elevation, and terrain required
Plate tectonics: the lithosphere is divided into rigid plates moving on the asthenosphere — the unifying theory of geology
Magnetic anomalies record geomagnetic reversals in oceanic crust — the Vine-Matthews-Morley hypothesis confirms seafloor spreading
Heat flow: Earth's interior (core ~6000°C) is hotter than its surface; convection in the mantle drives plate motion
Isostasy: the lithosphere floats on the asthenosphere — mountains have deep roots (Airy) or density variations (Pratt)
Seismic tomography: arrival-time inversions reveal 3D velocity structure — analogous to medical CT scanning of the Earth
reasoning_patterns: Seismic interpretation: trace reflectors on seismic sections, identify structural traps, distinguish lithology and fluid content
Rock physics: link elastic properties (Vp, Vs, density) to porosity, lithology, and pore fluid content (Gassmann, Biot theory)
Gravity/magnetic modeling: forward model subsurface bodies from known geometry and density; invert observed anomalies for geometry
Well log analysis: integrate multiple logs (gamma ray, density, neutron, resistivity, sonic) to characterize formations
Plate reconstruction: use magnetic isochrons, hot spot tracks, and paleomagnetism to reconstruct past plate configurations
Earthquake seismology: locate hypocenters from arrival times; compute moment tensors from waveform inversion
Geodetic monitoring: use GPS and InSAR to measure surface deformation at mm-scale precision
boundary_conditions: Deep Earth (> 2000 km) is poorly constrained seismically — resolution decreases with depth
Does not cover petroleum engineering (reservoir simulation, production optimization)
Does not cover mining geology or mineral exploration in full detail
Climate-geology interactions are cross-disciplinary between geophysics, oceanography, and atmospheric science
Core dynamics and geodynamo theory remain incompletely understood (why does the field reverse?)
key_references: Stein & Wysession. An Introduction to Seismology, Earthquakes, and Earth Structure
canonical_truths: Earth is ~4.54 billion years old — established by radiometric dating of meteorites and oldest Earth rocks
The outer core is liquid (S-waves do not propagate); the inner core is solid (PKJKP phases confirm)
Seismic tomography reveals downwelling slabs and upwelling plumes in the mantle — slab pull drives plate tectonics
Continental drift rate: ~2-10 cm/year — directly measured by GPS geodesy
P-wave velocity in crust: ~5-7 km/s; in mantle: ~8-13 km/s; in core: ~8-11 km/s
Earth's magnetic field reverses irregularly every ~200,000-300,000 years on average
